title: Reaction rate — the ledger in time
definition: The **reaction rate** is how fast the reaction extent advances — the time derivative of the species ledger. For a reactant A with coefficient $a$, the extent-normalized rate is $-\tfrac{1}{a}\tfrac{d[\mathrm{A}]}{dt}$ (divided by $a$ so one number describes the whole reaction); it is the same $d\xi/dt$ that stoichiometry, thermochemistry, and equilibrium all view differently — here watched *in time*. **Two conventions coexist**, differing by the factor $a$: that extent-normalized rate, and the **rate of disappearance of a chosen species**, $-\tfrac{d[\mathrm{A}]}{dt}$. The kinetics lessons here follow one reactant, so their measured rate law $\text{rate}=k[\mathrm{A}]^n$ and its integrated form use the per-species rate $-\tfrac{d[\mathrm{A}]}{dt}=k[\mathrm{A}]^n$ — the tabulated $k$ is that species' disappearance constant. A rate is **positive and changes as the reaction proceeds** (usually slowing as reactants deplete). How the rate depends on concentration is not read off the balanced equation — it is the empirically measured **rate law** (see [[rate-law]]). Equilibrium is the limit where the forward and reverse rates become equal, so the ledger stops moving.
